<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-begin -->
<script src="./js/display-prefs.js"></script>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-end -->
</head>
rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Moduli problems 2 The j-line 3 Fine vs. coarse 4 Why fine fails 5 Level structure 6 $M_g$ and $M_{g,n}$ 7 Mental model

Moduli spaces: the j-line, fine vs. coarse, and $M_{g,n}$

A moduli space packages "all objects of type $X$ up to isomorphism" into a single geometric object. We start where it works cleanest — the $j$-line classifying elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$ — watch the classical setup fail to be fine, see how level structures rescue it, and end at the moduli of curves $M_{g,n}$ and its Deligne–Mumford compactification.

Companion pages: Elliptic curves for the $j$-invariant itself, Schemes for representability, and a forward link to Stacks for the groupoid-valued fix.

blocks.html:
1. What is a moduli problem?

Informally, a moduli problem asks: is there a space whose points are the objects of type $X$, up to isomorphism? The word "space" is deliberately vague — we will sharpen it. The running example here is

$$\text{type } X \;=\; \text{elliptic curves over } \mathbb{C}, \qquad \text{isomorphism} \;=\; \text{isomorphism of algebraic curves with marked origin.}$$

The set of such curves, up to iso, is huge — but it admits a remarkably tidy parameter. Over $\mathbb{C}$, the $j$-invariant gives a bijection

$$j\colon \{\text{elliptic curves}/\mathbb{C}\}/\!\cong \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\; \mathbb{A}^1(\mathbb{C}) = \mathbb{C}.$$

So the set of iso classes is in bijection with the set of complex numbers. The moduli problem asks more: can we do this geometrically, so that the parameter space "feels" like a variety, and so that a family of curves over a base $B$ corresponds to a morphism $B \to \mathbb{A}^1$?

Point-of-view shift. Classical geometry starts with a space and asks about its points. Moduli theory starts with a class of objects and tries to build a space whose points are those objects. Whether such a space exists, and in what category, is the subtle part.

Formally, we attach to the moduli problem a functor $$\mathcal{M}\colon \mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Set}, \qquad \mathcal{M}(S) = \{\text{families of objects of type } X \text{ over } S\} / \cong_S.$$ A family over $S$ is a flat, proper morphism $E \to S$ whose geometric fibres are objects of type $X$. The moduli problem is representable if there is a scheme $M$ with $\mathcal{M}(-) \cong h_M(-) = \operatorname{Hom}(-,M)$. This is the gold standard; we call $M$ a fine moduli space.

See also
  • Representability and moduli functors

Used in
Moduli spaces · Fine versus coarse moduli

2. The j-line

For elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$, every iso class has a Weierstrass model $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$ with $\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27b^2) \ne 0$. The j-invariant $$j(E) \;=\; 1728 \cdot \frac{4a^3}{4a^3 + 27b^2}$$ is a complete invariant over an algebraically closed field: two curves are $\mathbb{C}$-isomorphic iff they have the same $j$.

Below: slide $j$ and see a representative Weierstrass curve deform. The real locus $\{y^2 = x^3 + ax + b\} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ changes shape as we move through $j$-values.

params.title: W1 · j-invariant slider

blocks.html:
The two special values:

  • $j = 0$ corresponds to curves $y^2 = x^3 + b$ (for instance $y^2 = x^3 + 1$). The extra automorphism $(x,y) \mapsto (\omega x,\,-y)$ with $\omega = e^{2\pi i / 3}$ has order $6$: $\operatorname{Aut}(E) \cong \mathbb{Z}/6$.
  • $j = 1728$ corresponds to $y^2 = x^3 + ax$ (for instance $y^2 = x^3 + x$). The extra automorphism $(x,y) \mapsto (-x,\,i y)$ has order $4$: $\operatorname{Aut}(E) \cong \mathbb{Z}/4$.
  • At every other $j$, the only non-trivial automorphism is $[-1]\colon (x,y) \mapsto (x,-y)$, so $\operatorname{Aut}(E) \cong \mathbb{Z}/2$.

params.title: W2 · special points exhibitor

blocks.html:
3. Fine versus coarse moduli

Take the moduli functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(S) = \{\text{elliptic curves } E \to S\}/\cong_S$. There are two levels of "solution."

Fine moduli

$M$ is a fine moduli space for $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ if $\mathcal{M}_{1,1} \cong h_M$ as functors. Taking $S = M$ and the identity in $\operatorname{Hom}(M,M) \cong \mathcal{M}_{1,1}(M)$ produces a universal family $\mathcal{E} \to M$ such that every family $E \to S$ is the pullback of $\mathcal{E}$ along a unique morphism $S \to M$.

Coarse moduli

$M$ is a coarse moduli space if we have a natural transformation $\varphi\colon \mathcal{M}_{1,1} \to h_M$ that is (a) a bijection on $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}(\operatorname{Spec} k) \to M(k)$ for every algebraically closed field $k$, and (b) universal among such natural transformations to a scheme. Coarse says: points match, but there is no universal family.

The $j$-line is the coarse moduli space of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ — not fine. The bijection on geometric points is exactly the $j$-invariant. Below: an interactive diagram of the two situations.

params.title: W3 · fine vs. coarse diagram

blocks.html:
Used in
Moduli spaces · Automorphisms obstruct fine moduli
Moduli spaces · Higher-genus moduli and stable curves

4. Why fine moduli fails for $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$

The obstruction to representability is automorphisms. Here is the mechanism in a sentence: if an object has a non-trivial automorphism, one can twist a trivial family into a non-trivial family whose fibres are still isomorphic to the original, and a representable functor cannot see this.

Concretely, consider the two families over the affine line $\mathbb{A}^1 = \operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Q}[t]$:

$$E_1: \; y^2 = x^3 + 1, \qquad E_2: \; y^2 = x^3 + t^6.$$

Over every $\bar{\mathbb{Q}}$-point $t = t_0 \ne 0$, both fibres have $j = 0$ and are isomorphic (via $x \mapsto t_0^2 x$, $y \mapsto t_0^3 y$). So they are pointwise the same family. But they are not isomorphic as families over $\mathbb{A}^1 \setminus \{0\}$: the isomorphism $x \mapsto t^2 x$, $y \mapsto t^3 y$ is not defined globally, and the obstruction to gluing lives in

$$H^1\!\left(\mathbb{A}^1 \setminus \{0\},\,\underline{\operatorname{Aut}}(E_1)\right).$$

If $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ were representable, the classifying maps of $E_1$ and $E_2$ would both be the constant map to $j = 0$, and so the families would be equal — but they are not. Contradiction.

params.title: W4 · twist widget
params.trailingExplainer: Left: $E_1 = \{y^2 = x^3+1\}$ (fibre at $t$). Right: $E_2 = \{y^2 = x^3+t^6\}$ (fibre at $t$). Both have $j = 0$; both are iso as abstract curves at every $t \ne 0$. But the iso requires the sixth-root twist $x \mapsto t^2 x$, $y \mapsto t^3 y$, which is a non-trivial class in $H^1$.

blocks.html: The same phenomenon happens at $j = 1728$ (quartic twist) and more mildly at every $j$ (quadratic twist from $[-1]$). The mass of automorphisms at $j = 0$ and $j = 1728$ makes $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ an orbifold / Deligne–Mumford stack with extra structure at those two points.

Used in
Moduli spaces · Level structure and compactification ideas

5. Rigidifying with level structure

A level-$N$ structure on an elliptic curve $E$ is an isomorphism $\alpha\colon (\mathbb{Z}/N)^2 \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} E[N]$, i.e. an ordered basis of the $N$-torsion. Equivalently: a pair of points $P, Q \in E[N]$ generating $E[N]$.

The key fact: for $N \ge 3$, no non-trivial automorphism of an elliptic curve fixes a level-$N$ structure. The functor

$$\mathcal{M}_{1,1}[N](S) \;=\; \{(E \to S,\,\alpha) : \text{level-}N \text{ structure}\}/\cong$$

is representable by a smooth affine variety $Y(N)$. The natural forgetful map $Y(N) \to \mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is finite étale (over the locus away from $j = 0, 1728$), with Galois group $\operatorname{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}/N)$.

This is the standard rigidification trick: add data that kills automorphisms, get fine moduli, then quotient to recover the original.

params.title: W5 · 2-torsion visualizer

blocks.html:
Why $N \ge 3$? For $N = 2$, every elliptic curve has automorphism $[-1]$, and $[-1]$ acts as the identity on $E[2]$ (because $-P = P$ for $2$-torsion). So a level-$2$ structure is not rigid enough — $[-1]$ still fixes every level-$2$ basis. For $N = 3$, $[-1]$ acts as $-1 \ne 1$ on $E[3]$, so it is detected.
See also
  • Definition of modular forms

6. Higher genus: $M_g$ and $M_{g,n}$

Let $M_g$ be the (coarse) moduli space of smooth projective curves of genus $g$, and $M_{g,n}$ the moduli of such curves with $n$ ordered distinct marked points. These exist as quasi-projective varieties (for $g \ge 2$; $M_{0,n}$ and $M_{1,n}$ require care).

Their dimensions:

$$\dim M_g \;=\; \begin{cases} 0 & g = 0,\\ 1 & g = 1,\\ 3g - 3 & g \ge 2, \end{cases} \qquad \dim M_{g,n} \;=\; \dim M_g + n.$$

The $3g-3$ comes from Riemann–Roch / deformation theory: $\dim H^1(C, T_C) = 3g - 3$ is the dimension of first-order deformations of a smooth curve of genus $g \ge 2$.

Compactification: stable curves

The open moduli $M_{g,n}$ is not compact — curves can degenerate. Deligne and Mumford (1969) enlarge to the moduli of stable curves, giving a smooth proper Deligne–Mumford stack $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}$ with coarse space $\overline{M}_{g,n}$.

A stable curve $(C, p_1, \dots, p_n)$ is a connected, proper, reduced, at-worst-nodal curve of arithmetic genus $g$ such that:

  • every smooth rational component contains at least $3$ special points (nodes + markings);
  • every genus-$1$ component contains at least $1$ special point.

Equivalently: $\operatorname{Aut}(C, p_1, \dots, p_n)$ is finite. Stability is exactly what is needed to cut out a Deligne–Mumford stack.

params.title: W6 · stable curve gallery

blocks.html:
See also
  • Divisors and the Riemann–Roch theorem

7. Mental model summary

A moduli problem is, at heart, a functor $$\mathcal{F}\colon \mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Set} \;\text{ or }\; \mathrm{Grpd}.$$ The question "does $\mathcal{F}$ come from a scheme?" has three graded answers.

levelconditionwhat you get
fine$\mathcal{F} \cong h_M$ as set-valued functorsscheme $M$ + universal family
coarse$\mathcal{F} \to h_M$ bijective on $\bar k$-points, universal into schemesscheme $M$, no universal family
stack$\mathcal{F}$ valued in groupoids, satisfies descentDeligne–Mumford or Artin stack

Automorphisms are the villain and the hero. They obstruct fine moduli (by making twists), but they are also the data that upgrades a coarse variety into a richer stack: the stack $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}$ remembers, at each curve $C$, the finite group $\operatorname{Aut}(C)$. The coarse space $\overline{M}_{g,n}$ is obtained by forgetting these groups.

The forward link: stacks is exactly the framework that lets us replace "functor to Set, mod out by iso, lose information" with "functor to Groupoids, remember the isos as data." The $j$-line then becomes the coarse space of the stack $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{1,1}$, with stacky points of order $6$ at $j = 0$ and order $4$ at $j = 1728$.

See also: Elliptic curves for $j$ and Weierstrass forms; Riemann surfaces for the $\mathbb{H}/\operatorname{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ picture of $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}^{\mathrm{an}}$; Schemes for representable functors; Sheaf cohomology for the $H^1$ of twists; Morphisms & fibre products for pullback-of-family statements.

Part of the Interactive Mathematics Notebook · open in a browser, no server needed
